title: Indistinguishability of photonic qubits emitted from trapped $^{40}$Ca$^+$ ions via pulsed excitation
authors: Pascal Baumgart, Max Bergerhoff, Jonas Meiers, Stephan Kucera, Jürgen Eschner
abstract: We investigate the indistinguishability of Raman photons generated from two trapped $^{40}$Ca$^+$ ions using few-nanosecond excitation pulses. We elucidate how spontaneous scattering back to the initial state affects Hong-Ou-Mandel interference. We identify the mean number of back-decays as a measurable single-emitter quantity that correlates with achievable interference visibility of photons from two identical emitters.
summary: This paper experimentally investigates the indistinguishability of single photons emitted from trapped $^{40}	ext{Ca}^+$ ions. The key finding is establishing a quantitative correlation between the mean number of spontaneous back-decays ($\langle N angle$) observed from one ion and the visibility of the Hong-Ou-Mandel interference when two ions are used. This relationship offers a simple, measurable diagnostic for optimizing quantum repeater segments.
main_problem: The study investigates how spontaneous scattering back to the initial state affects the indistinguishability of Raman photons generated from two trapped $^{40}	ext{Ca}^+$ ions, which is crucial for quantum entanglement protocols.
main_result: The mean number of back-decays ($\langle N angle$) from a single ion was found to be a measurable quantity that correlates inversely with the achievable Hong-Ou-Mandel (HOM) interference visibility ($V$).
method: The authors measured the arrival time distributions of photons and calculated $\langle N angle$ from single-emitter statistics, and measured $V$ using two co-trapped ions interfering on a beam splitter.
model_or_system: The physical system is trapped $^{40}	ext{Ca}^+$ ions excited by pulsed lasers to generate Raman photons (854 nm). The process is limited by spontaneous decay back to the ground state, which degrades photon indistinguishability.
key_observables: HOM visibility ($V$), mean number of back-decays ($\langle N angle$), and photon success probability ($P_{854}$).
important_parameters: Excitation pulse length ($T_{	ext{pulse}}$) in the few-nanosecond range, and the repetition time ($T_{	ext{rep}}$) for two-photon interference.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that $\langle N angle$ provides a reliable diagnostic tool for the HOM visibility, and that certain experimental imperfections (like polarization mismatch) can be accounted for or are secondary to the primary correlation.
figures_summary: Figures show arrival time distributions of 393-nm and 854-nm photons, and critically, plot the anti-correlation between HOM visibility ($V(T_{	ext{rep}})$) and the measured $\langle N angle$ and $P_{854}$.
paper_structure: The paper details the experimental setup using pulsed excitation, analyzes single-emitter back-decay statistics to quantify $\langle N angle$, and then measures two-emitter HOM interference to establish the quantitative relationship between $\langle N angle$ and $V$.
why_interesting: This work provides a direct, measurable diagnostic tool ($\langle N angle$) derived from single-emitter measurements that can predict the performance (HOM visibility) of a complex two-emitter quantum process, which is highly valuable for quantum networking.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Arrival time distribution of 393-nm (blue) and 854- nm (red) detection events when the ion is excited by a train of 80 pulses of pulse length Tpulse = 15.1(1) ns and spacing Trep ≈104.25 ns, reconstructed from 134 × 106 cycles of the experimental sequence. Inset: Close-up of the distribution in a time interval corresponding to a single excitation pulse, where the number of 393-nm detection events has been scaled down by a factor of ten to match the heights of the two peaks. Shown in black is the shape of the excitation pulse, recon- structed as outlined in appendix A.

FIG. 3. Distribution of detected back-decay photons at 393 nm for excitation with a pulse length of Tpulse = 15.1(1) ns, reconstructed from 134 × 106 cycles of the exper- imental sequence. The measured cross-correlation between 854-nm and 393-nm photons is shown in blue and compared with a numerical simulation, which is shown as a black line that was scaled to have the same area as the corresponding peak in the measured data. The gray shaded region indicates the integration interval used to determine the mean number of back-decays for this measurement. Its length equals half the pulse repetition period of Trep ≈104.25 ns.

FIG. 4. Mean number of back-decays for different excitation pulse lengths Tpulse (FWHM of the intensity profile). The measured data (black) is compared with numerical simula- tions (blue) that use the measured pulse shape and success probability P854 (red) as input parameters. A detailed ac- count of the experimental parameters is given in Table I in appendix A.

FIG. 6. Detection time difference distributions of 854-nm two-photon detection events for ion state readouts projecting onto orthogonal (red) or parallel (blue) polarizations, for an excitation pulse length of Tpulse = 31.7(1) ns. The distribu- tion is reconstructed from 357 × 107 cycles of the experimen- tal sequence. The measured data is shown as solid lines and compared with numerical simulations (dashed lines) using ex- perimentally determined parameters for the excitation pulse.

FIG. 7. HOM visibility V (T) in dependence of the co- incidence window size T for three different pulse lengths Tpulse = 15.8(1) ns (blue), Tpulse = 31.7(1) ns (green) and Tpulse = 55.3(2) ns (red). The measurement results are shown as solid lines, their uncertainties as shaded regions around the data points. Numerical calculations using experimental pa- rameters are shown as dashed lines. Single highlighted data points (circles with uncertainty bars) mark the values V (Trep) evaluated at the pulse repetition period.
